title: Milan — Aegis AI Log Intelligence walkthrough
description: Frontend-verified end-to-end tour of the Aegis.ai read-only Log Intelligence dashboard, walking the IT Admin's five implemented surfaces — the Home desktop with its clock, calendar, and notifications widgets; the Dashboard with four KPI tiles, the twenty-four-hour Login Activity bar chart, the Risk Distribution donut, and the live Event Feed; the Alerts table with six analyzed columns; the combined Summary surface stacking Sources, Policy Findings, and Reports; and the append-only Audit Log — all driven by GET-only polling against the analyzed log baseline served by milan-be.

meta.author: Milan
meta.pacingNote: Pauses kept short. TTS adds ~500ms of startup latency per utterance, and the dashboard has five surfaces to cover — long pauses make it drag. Tab switches are a single click on the bottom dock; there is no hamburger to open first.

steps.description: Opening line — no UI action. Sets the persona (Milan, the read-only log analysis companion) and frames the system: Aegis ingests CSV log baselines, the agent normalizes and scores them, and the IT Admin reviews everything in this dashboard. Final Accept or Reject decisions are handled out-of-band over email.

speak.before: Hello. I am Milan, your read-only log analysis companion on the Aegis desk. Let me walk you through what the dashboard is surfacing for the latest analyzed CSV batch — and where the IT Admin's eyes need to land before the next ingestion cycle.

steps.description: Frame the Aegis Home desktop — the system entry point with three live widgets pinned to the surface. No app window is open at this point.

speak.before: We start on the Aegis Home — the desktop you see before opening any app.
speak.after: Three live widgets sit on the desktop: the time-of-day clock with a contextual greeting, today's date with a six-row calendar grid, and a notifications card surfacing the most recent analyzed anomalies. Nothing is editable on Home; it is a read-only landing surface.

steps.description: Spotlight the clock widget — the live wall-clock readout pinned top-left of the desktop, refreshing every second.

speak.before: On the left, the live clock widget.
speak.after: Twelve-hour time, AM/PM indicator, and a contextual greeting that swings from late night to good morning to good evening across the day. The dashboard itself refreshes against this same wall-clock baseline whenever you switch tabs.

steps.description: Spotlight the notifications widget — preview of the latest analyzed alerts with a click-to-open modal for the full list.

speak.before: Below the date, the Notifications card.
speak.after: Total alert count up top plus a short preview of the two newest anomalies — user, source, and the headline reason. Clicking it opens the full modal — but the table view on the Alerts screen is the source of truth, so we use the dock to head there.

steps.description: Open the Dashboard from the bottom dock — single click on the Dashboard tab; the app window scales up from the dock icon.

speak.before: Let us open the Dashboard — the working analytical surface.
speak.after: Metrics, charts, and event feed from the analyzed security log baseline. The page label pill in the header reads Log Analysis to confirm we are looking at the analyzed slice, not raw ingest.

steps.description: Spotlight the four KPI tiles row at the top of the Dashboard — the five-second pipeline-health read.

speak.before: The headline KPI row carries four metrics summarizing this batch.
speak.after: Total Events analyzed, Cleaned Records normalized to the schema, Duplicates Removed by exact-row match, and Anomalies Detected at risk score forty-five or above. Together they answer: how big was the batch, how clean was it, and what fraction of it is risky.

steps.description: Spotlight the Anomalies Detected KPI — the load-bearing tile that must equal the Risk Distribution donut total on the right.

speak.before: Anomalies Detected is the load-bearing tile — the tile coloured warn whenever the count is non-zero.
speak.after: Risk score threshold is forty-five. That number must reconcile with the Risk Distribution donut total to the right; if it does not, the analysis pipeline has dropped or duplicated a row and I should refuse to vouch for the batch.

steps.description: Frame the Login Activity bar chart — twenty-four hourly bins of login volume across the analyzed window.

speak.before: Login Activity — a twenty-four-hour window of logins across the analyzed baseline, hour by hour.
speak.after: I read the peak hour and the rough shape of the curve only. Each bar carries its own tooltip with the exact event count, but I will not invent values for bars I cannot see directly.

steps.description: Frame the Risk Distribution donut — the four-segment severity mix that must reconcile with the Anomalies KPI.

speak.before: Risk Distribution — anomalies broken down by severity, in a four-segment donut.
speak.after: Low in green, Medium in cyan, High in amber, Critical in red. The four legend values must sum to the Anomalies Detected count on the left — that reconciliation is the integrity check on the entire scoring step.

steps.description: Frame the Event Feed — the live ticker of latest normalized security events at the bottom of the Dashboard.

speak.before: Below the charts, the Event Feed lists the latest normalized security events from the analyzed batch.
speak.during: Each row carries a coloured status dot, the user, the event type and outcome, the severity tag, and a timestamp.
speak.after: The ANALYZED badge in the header confirms this slice reflects the latest batch; if the badge ever flips, the underlying API has not yet finished a refresh and I will say so.

steps.description: Switch to the Alerts page for the full anomaly table — single dock click, no hamburger.

speak.before: Now Alerts.
speak.after: Analyzed anomalies with risk scores, rationales, and review status — the source of truth for the IT Admin's email Accept or Reject decision. The dock badge over the icon counts open alerts; opening the page itself does not change that count.

steps.description: Frame the Anomaly Alerts table — six analyzed columns, one row per flagged event.

speak.before: These anomalies are flagged based on deviation from baseline behaviour at risk score forty-five or above.
speak.after: Six columns: Timestamp, User, Event, Risk Score, Reason, and Status. The risk-score chip is colour-coded by severity, and the status pill cycles through pending, approved, and dismissed as the email decisions land on the next ingestion cycle.

steps.description: Spotlight the top anomaly row — typically the highest-risk Critical entry, ranked by the analyzer.

speak.before: Here is the top row — usually the highest-risk entry in the batch.
speak.during: Risk score, severity colour on the chip, and the rationale that earned the flag.
speak.after: Status remains pending until the IT Admin's email Accept or Reject decision flips the pill on the next ingestion cycle. Nothing about that flip happens from this UI — this is read-only.

steps.description: Switch to the Summary tab — Sources, Policy Findings, and Reports stacked vertically into a single combined surface.

speak.before: Next, Summary.
speak.after: Sources health, policy findings, and report status — combined into one read-only surface, three cards stacked top-to-bottom, each carrying its own ANALYZED badge.

steps.description: Frame the Sources card on the Summary screen — per-source-family health summary across the analyzed baseline.

speak.before: Top of Summary, the Sources card.
speak.after: One tile per source family represented in the analyzed baseline — Identity, Endpoint, SSO, ERP, and any other system the ingest pipeline saw — each with its event count and a one-line analysis comment summarising what that family contributed to this batch.

steps.description: Frame the Policy Findings card — compliance and policy violations inferred from the analyzed events.

speak.before: Below Sources, Policy Findings.
speak.after: Compliance and policy findings inferred from the analyzed log events — violations, privileged events, and blocked activity, each with a count and a one-line rationale. These are surfaced here, not enforced from the UI; enforcement still lives in the originating system.

steps.description: Frame the Reports card at the bottom of the Summary screen — static summaries generated from the analyzed activity.

speak.before: And at the bottom, the Reports card.
speak.after: Static summaries generated from the analyzed activity — Daily Risk, Incident, and Trend — each marked Ready with a one-line headline and a status dot. These are deterministic outputs of the analyzed batch, not editable narratives.

steps.description: Switch to the Audit Log for the chronological workspace activity timeline.

speak.before: Audit Log next.
speak.after: Read-only trace of analysis refreshes, anomaly detections, and dashboard review activity — append-only, never edited, never re-ordered.

steps.description: Frame the Workspace Activity timeline — every recorded event newest-first, with type-coloured icons.

speak.before: The Workspace Activity timeline holds the recent audit events, newest first.
speak.during: Daily security reports, exfiltration risks, anomaly detections, ingestion resumes, and privilege escalation alerts — each with its own coloured icon by type.
speak.after: Mixing analyses, ingestions, and reports is intentional — one chronological story of what the system observed and when. This is the trail compliance pulls when they need to reconstruct the lifecycle of a single analyzed batch.

steps.description: Return to the Dashboard to close the tour — single dock click takes us back to the working analytical surface.

speak.before: Coming back to the Dashboard.
speak.after: That is the full picture — Home, Dashboard, Alerts, Summary, and Audit. Five read-only surfaces, one analyzed batch, and the IT Admin in the loop via email for the only mutating decisions Aegis ever makes.

steps.description: Closing line — no UI action. Hands the mic back to the listener.

speak.before: Final Accept or Reject decisions are taken via email and will be reflected on the next ingestion cycle. Anything on those five screens you would like me to dig into?
